title: Sunrise, Sunset

jokes.text: John orders just the 'little farmer's breakfast' to save his appetite for the wedding tasting, framing it as virtuous restraint.
jokes.setup: John orders a small breakfast, claiming he doesn't want to spoil his appetite for the wedding tasting.
jokes.punchline: The joke seeds his later gluttony at the tasting — the 'restraint' is immediately undercut by his inability to resist food.
jokes.explanation: Mild but effective character setup. John's self-deceiving frugality is a running theme and this plants the seed for his tasting behavior. The rewatch value is high because the payoff comes later.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: If you have to keep telling people that you're giving them something, then you're not really giving them anything.

jokes.text: Moira pointedly asks 'Yes, John. Why wouldn't you have Twyla cater David's big day?' — dripping with sarcasm about John's cheapness.
jokes.setup: Twyla asks why the café isn't catering the wedding; John awkwardly deflects.
jokes.punchline: Moira zeroes in with theatrical sarcasm, exposing John's frugality in front of everyone.
jokes.explanation: Very Moira — she weaponizes politeness as a knife. The sarcasm is character-specific and lands cleanly. The joke does real comedic work by exposing the family dynamic.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, you've done it. You've invited Twyla to the wedding. Do you realize what you've done? You've become a Turkish Saint Nicholas, bestowing gifts upon the deserving and undeserving alike with reckless abandon.

jokes.text: 'It's just the more you keep mentioning that it's a gift, the less and less it feels like a gift.'
jokes.setup: John has been repeatedly emphasizing that the wedding food is 'his gift to David.'
jokes.punchline: David dryly punctures the performative generosity — the more you advertise a gift, the less generous it seems.
jokes.explanation: This is a sharp, well-structured observation delivered with perfect David deadpan. It captures a genuine social truth (performative generosity undermines itself) and is entirely character-specific — only David would say this so precisely and dryly. High quotability and great economy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So you solved one problem and created two others. That's actually impressive, Dad.

jokes.text: John's awkward pivot: 'I-I was just thinking that, you know, how can Twyla enjoy herself at the wedding if she's-if she's working?' — a transparent rationalization.
jokes.setup: Caught by David's observation and Moira's sarcasm, John scrambles for a reason why Twyla wasn't invited.
jokes.punchline: His stammering, obviously reverse-engineered justification is more damning than silence would have been.
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy — John's 'generous' explanations always dig him deeper. The stammering delivery implied by the text adds to it. Solid but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Tippy Bernstein has her fingers in so many irons in the fire.

jokes.text: 'I'm invited to the wedding?' — Twyla's genuinely surprised, innocent reaction to learning she's invited.
jokes.setup: John's improvised excuse inadvertently implies Twyla IS invited.
jokes.punchline: Twyla's completely guileless surprise — she had no idea — makes John's original slight even worse retroactively.
jokes.explanation: Twyla's oblivious warmth is perfectly deployed. Four words does tremendous work: it reveals she wasn't invited, makes John's lie more absurd, and gets a genuine laugh from Twyla's unguarded sweetness. High economy score justified.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You look absolutely radiant today.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Mom, I look terrible.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I know, but I'm trying to build your confidence.

jokes.text: 'Look at you, John, doling out gift after gift this morning like the Turkish Saint Nikolaas.'
jokes.setup: John has accidentally extended an invitation to Twyla on top of paying for the food.
jokes.punchline: Moira's grandiose, oddly specific 'Turkish Saint Nikolaas' comparison is both praise and mockery — and the specificity of 'Turkish' is pure Moira absurdism.
jokes.explanation: Only Moira Rose would say 'Turkish Saint Nikolaas.' The specificity is the joke — it's not Santa Claus, it's not even Saint Nicholas, it's the Turkish variant, delivered with utter conviction. This is peak Moira: simultaneously complimenting and condescending, wrapped in absurd erudition. High character integration and originality are fully justified.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Hello, I'm Johnny Rose, owner of the Rosebud Motel.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Yeah, we know. It's on all the books you're handing out.

jokes.text: Moira dramatically announces 'I must attend to some good news of my own' and exits, leaving everyone hanging.
jokes.setup: David asks Moira what her good news is.
jokes.punchline: Rather than sharing, Moira makes her exit into a theatrical event, treating her departure as performance.
jokes.explanation: Solid Moira character beat — her inability to share the spotlight even with her own good news is funny. Not a standout but contributes to her characterization.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Welcome to the Rose Apothecary. I'm Roland Schitt, co-owner of this establishment along with Mr. Rose here.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Now, I know what you're thinking — 'Roland, how did you become co-owner?' Well, let me tell you, it's all about reading the right books.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I just finished 'The Art of the Deal' and let me tell you, I am the deal now.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: In fact, Mr. Rose, I've decided that from now on, all major decisions go through me first.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, we discussed this—

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: The book says the co-owner needs to establish dominance. I'm establishing dominance.

jokes.text: Upon seeing Alexis looking rough: 'Oh, you're looking so natural and peppy.' — Moira's performative compliment that is clearly a lie.
jokes.setup: Alexis arrives looking terrible after a rough night.
jokes.punchline: Moira deploys an obviously false compliment with complete sincerity, which is somehow more insulting.
jokes.explanation: Moira's backhanded compliments are a reliable comedic device. 'Natural and peppy' is a funny choice of words. Functional and character-specific but not a standout.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, that's just great. Roland, thank you for that incredibly helpful introduction. I'm sure the new employees now have a very clear understanding of the business structure here.

jokes.text: Well, you're fooling everyone, dear.
jokes.setup: Alexis confirms she had a rough night; Johnny tried to compliment her.
jokes.punchline: Moira's response acknowledges the lie while framing it as genuine encouragement — delivered with total sincerity that makes it more cutting.
jokes.explanation: Short, devastating, perfectly Moira. She delivers a non-compliment as if it's a compliment, and the economy is exceptional. Four words that say everything about Moira's relationship with artifice.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah, so... the east wing of the motel basically collapsed last night.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: We're looking at a six-figure repair bill, the guests are threatening lawsuits, and the foundation is apparently made of the same material as a rice cake.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: But other than that, things are great.

jokes.text: John notices something in Alexis's hair and awkwardly pulls it out — the visual beat of Alexis asking 'Is it gone?' and the moment of shared discomfort.
jokes.setup: Alexis has had a rough night; something is in her hair.
jokes.punchline: The physical awkwardness of a parent picking something out of their adult daughter's hair in public, followed by her forlorn 'Is it gone?'
jokes.explanation: Gentle physical comedy. The pathos of Alexis's messy state and John's quiet dad-care gives it warmth. Not a big laugh but a charming character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, you know, I've been thinking about donating one of my organs. Tax write-off.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: The thing is, my liver's not gonna fetch much after all these years.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: And my kidneys? They don't perform like they used to.

jokes.text: Roland interrupting Johnny's introduction: 'It's printed right here on the cover of the book that he's handing out to all of you.'
jokes.setup: Johnny formally introduces himself to new motel staff.
jokes.punchline: Roland undercuts the moment by literally pointing out his name is already on the materials — making the introduction feel pompous.
jokes.explanation: Classic Roland deflation. It's both rude and technically accurate — a good combination. Shows the motel partnership dynamic efficiently.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, you could always have an 'accidental' fire. You know, completely by chance.

jokes.text: Roland introduces himself as 'the boss you're gonna come to when you don't understand why this guy is making you read about VHS tapes.'
jokes.setup: Roland introduces himself after undermining Johnny.
jokes.punchline: He positions himself as the anti-Johnny while simultaneously confessing his own ignorance about VHS tapes and Johnny's business philosophy.
jokes.explanation: The VHS tapes detail is a funny specific — Johnny's business book references outdated media metaphors and Roland is gleefully ignorant of it. Good character work that also plants a payoff joke later.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We're not selling organs.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, that's good to know, because my kidney donation was just rejected.

jokes.text: 'Not exactly the united front we had discussed, Roland.'
jokes.setup: Roland has completely sabotaged Johnny's staff orientation.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's masterfully restrained, passive-aggressive understatement.
jokes.explanation: Johnny's long-suffering politeness is a reliable source of comedy. 'Not exactly' is doing tremendous work — it's the absolute minimum protest, which makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh my God, look at her. She's being so rude to that woman. That is just unnecessary.

jokes.text: Stevie reveals the termite damage and foundation collapse in front of all the new staff.
jokes.setup: Johnny tries to redirect Stevie to speak privately; she says 'Okay' and then immediately announces the crisis out loud.
jokes.punchline: The catastrophic news is delivered to the entire assembled staff who were just being welcomed to the 'Rosebud Motel family.'
jokes.explanation: The timing is excellent — Johnny's 'we're all family here' line sets it up perfectly, and Stevie's unsentimental honesty demolishes the orientation's positive energy. Stevie as the anti-pretension voice is well-used.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: But if Sheila already knew that Marcus was the father, why would she sleep with his brother?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Because, darling, television is not a medium that concerns itself with logic or consistency. It's about the emotional truth of a moment.

jokes.text: Johnny sends the new staff to the vending machine while a crisis meeting happens — 'Why don't we take five, and feel free to help yourself to one of our vending machine over there.'
jokes.setup: Johnny needs to salvage the orientation while managing a financial disaster.
jokes.punchline: The grand 'Rosebud Motel family welcome' devolves to pointing people toward a vending machine.
jokes.explanation: Good deflation gag. The visual of new employees shuffling to a vending machine while management argues about organ sales is funny context, but this specific line is just the setup vehicle.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I want to come to the meeting with you.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Absolutely not.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Why not? I could be helpful.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: The last time you came to a meeting, you called the producer 'Mr. Dildo.'

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: His name is Dilbert! I'm not trying to be funny!

jokes.text: 'Well, I gave one of my kidneys to my cousin Bruce. Uh, we can see how I run on an empty tank.'
jokes.setup: The team is brainstorming how to cover the cost of the foundation repairs.
jokes.punchline: Roland casually reveals he already donated a kidney and uses it as a financial reference point — framing organ donation as an asset class.
jokes.explanation: Classic Roland — the casual reveal that he already gave away a kidney (to cousin Bruce!) is wonderfully absurd and specific. 'Run on an empty tank' is a perfect piece of misapplied idiom. Only Roland would frame organ donation as a business resource he already spent.

dialogue.line: The inherently ridiculous nature of soap opera plotting

jokes.text: 'I went to school with a guy who starts "accidental" fires.'
jokes.setup: They need money fast; options are running out.
jokes.punchline: Stevie casually floats arson as a financial solution, the air quotes around 'accidental' making it even more incriminating.
jokes.explanation: Excellent Stevie joke. The deadpan delivery of what is essentially a confession of arson connections, the air quotes doing all the work, and the casual 'went to school with' framing — this is peak Stevie nihilism. High economy, high originality for the show, and perfectly character-integrated. Genuinely memorable line.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You know what? Maybe I won't pay for the food after all.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Dad!

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm kidding. I'll pay for it. You know why? Because despite the fact that you're difficult, and demanding, and frankly, quite monstrous most of the time... you're not a monster all of the time.

jokes.text: 'We're not burning anything down and we're not selling any organs.' — Johnny having to explicitly rule out arson and organ trafficking.
jokes.setup: Roland and Stevie have each offered genuinely illegal/inadvisable solutions.
jokes.punchline: Johnny has to formally veto two criminal acts as if they were legitimate boardroom proposals.
jokes.explanation: The escalation pays off beautifully — the fact that both arson and organ sales need to be vetoed in one breath is the joke. Johnny as the reasonable straight man vetoing insane proposals is a great recurring device.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: We're late. We're officially late. Do you know what this means? The caterer now thinks we're the kind of people who are late to things. We'll be remembered as the late people. They'll tell other caterers about us.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: David, we're three minutes late.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Three minutes might as well be three hours in the catering world. We've probably lost our deposit. They've definitely given our table to another couple. A couple who respects time.

jokes.text: 'That's good, because Bruce told me my kidney was garbage.'
jokes.setup: Johnny has ruled out organ selling; Roland's kidney donation was established earlier.
jokes.punchline: Roland reveals his kidney was rejected — with the specific, humanizing detail that 'Bruce told me my kidney was garbage.'
jokes.explanation: This is the standout joke of the episode's first half. The callback structure is perfect: plant the kidney in joke #17, let Johnny veto it in #19, then detonate the callback with Bruce's verdict. 'My kidney was garbage' is an extraordinarily specific, absurd, and quotable piece of character writing. The specificity of Bruce personally critiquing the kidney quality is inspired. This comfortably reaches the 8+ threshold — it's comparable to the 'Snip snap' anchor in escalation and quotability, though not quite as iconic.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Everyone, I'd like you to meet our caterer. Stevie's here too—she just doesn't have a job, so she eats.

jokes.text: Alexis watching 'Sunrise Bay' on the box set: 'Take the pills, Vivian. / A "please" might be nice!'
jokes.setup: Alexis is watching her mother's old soap opera on DVD.
jokes.punchline: The soap opera dialogue is deliberately melodramatic, and 'a please might be nice' adds a whiff of Moira's real personality into the fictional character.
jokes.explanation: Fun meta moment — the soap dialogue is a gentle parody of the genre, and the demanding attitude bleeds between Vivian and Moira nicely.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: That's a beautiful salad. Very fresh. The greens are really... vibrant.

dialogue.speaker: Penelope
dialogue.line: It's garnish.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm sorry?

dialogue.speaker: Penelope
dialogue.line: It's garnish. For the plate.

jokes.text: 'You really think you'd be head of surgery at Sunrise General if you weren't possessed by your father? Who also happened to be the former head of surgery at Sunrise General?'
jokes.setup: The show-within-a-show soap opera dialogue is being watched on DVD.
jokes.punchline: The gleefully convoluted soap opera logic — nepotism even in supernatural possession — is a sharp parody of the genre's ridiculous escalation.
jokes.explanation: The soap opera parody lands well — the logic of 'possessed by nepotism' is genuinely funny and a good send-up of how soaps work. The nested absurdity (supernatural career advancement) is a smart comedic concept.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Lobster rolls? Do you know how much lobster costs? I once bought a small island just to avoid thinking about prices like that.

jokes.text: 'Oh, enjoying the box set, are we? This season is weird. You're great in it, but I forgot how bad Clifton Sparks was.'
jokes.setup: Moira enters to find Alexis watching Sunrise Bay.
jokes.punchline: Moira's casual self-praise ('you're great in it') paired with her dismissal of a co-star she's about to meet — creating dramatic irony since we know Clifton is about to reappear.
jokes.explanation: Excellent dramatic irony — Moira casually trashing Clifton when he's literally coming to see her today creates a lovely dramatic irony payoff. Her reflexive self-complimenting ('you're great in it') is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, perhaps we could simply... arrange the garnish more artfully. A microgreen here, a radish ribbon there...

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We could call it a 'deconstructed salad.' Very trendy. Very expensive-sounding.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm quite certain no one will notice it's primarily parsley and a single cherry tomato.

jokes.text: 'Oh, it's Uncle Sparky to you, Dear.' — Moira defending Clifton Sparks as 'Uncle Sparky' after dismissing him.
jokes.setup: Alexis calls Clifton Sparks bad; Moira has just agreed.
jokes.punchline: Moira pivots to mild defensiveness by invoking his family nickname — revealing deeper complexity/history while staying in character.
jokes.explanation: 'Uncle Sparky' is a great character detail — implies a long, complicated history. Moira's pivot from critique to fond nickname in one breath is funny.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, if we're going to charge that much per plate, we need to invite people who actually expect that level of cuisine. I'm calling the Tulsa Country Club's most pretentious members.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: David, that's not how this works—

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Father, I'm doing you a favor. These people will pay anything if you make them feel exclusive enough.

jokes.text: Alexis's soap opera plot confusion: 'Why is Vivian the only one who gets possessed when Trystan also played the Ouija game?'
jokes.setup: Alexis is deep in the Sunrise Bay box set and engaging earnestly with its plot logic.
jokes.punchline: The sincere application of logical scrutiny to soap opera supernatural nonsense is funny; Alexis treating it like a solvable mystery.
jokes.explanation: Fun meta commentary. Alexis engaging with soap opera logic like it's a real mystery is character-appropriate and the Ouija detail is delightfully specific.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: This is... an interesting cut. You know, the key to a truly great beef tenderloin is understanding its provenance. Where does it come from? Who raised the cow? What were its hopes and dreams?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Because that, that matters. That matters tremendously when you're looking at this kind of... premium pricing.

jokes.text: Moira insists Alexis shouldn't come to the Tippy Bernstein meeting: 'not necessary, dear. Not in your condition.'
jokes.setup: Alexis, who looks terrible after a rough night, wants to come as Moira's publicist.
jokes.punchline: Moira's 'not in your condition' is simultaneously caring and brutally honest — she's protecting Moira's image by keeping Alexis hidden.
jokes.explanation: Good Moira-Alexis dynamic. The line reveals Moira's priorities (her image over her daughter's feelings) without being cruel — she frames it as caring.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: People shouldn't focus on the food.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, what? Dad, are you feeling okay? Did you hit your head?

jokes.text: 'What's important is that you stay here where no one can see you and focus on your critical self-care.'
jokes.setup: Moira is trying to keep the disheveled Alexis away from her important meeting.
jokes.punchline: 'Where no one can see you' is the actual reason, barely dressed up as concern for Alexis's wellbeing.
jokes.explanation: The tell is 'where no one can see you' — Moira's true concern slips through her performance of maternal care. Funny and revealing of character simultaneously.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Oh, a free sample. How generous. And what's the catch? That I'll suddenly feel obligated to buy the forty-dollar jar of whatever this is?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Because that's how these things work, isn't it? One complimentary morsel and suddenly I'm walking out with three hundred dollars worth of artisanal whatever.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: No thank you. I've learned my lesson. Free samples are just expensive purchases wearing a very thin disguise.

jokes.text: 'How dare you make me kill my father twice!' — Moira reacting dramatically to Sunrise Bay on TV.
jokes.setup: Moira is watching Sunrise Bay while preparing to leave.
jokes.punchline: The soap opera apparently has Moira's character killing her own father, twice, and she's outraged at the writing.
jokes.explanation: Brief but brilliant. The outrage is directed at the writing, not the act — Moira's problem is not 'patricide twice' but that the writers put her through it. The word 'twice' is doing enormous work. It tells us everything about both Sunrise Bay and Moira's relationship with the show. High economy, high character integration, genuinely quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Tippy
dialogue.line: My hypnotist cured my vertigo by making me believe I was a chicken. Now I can't stop clucking at heights.

dialogue.speaker: Tippy
dialogue.line: Then I married the hypnotist, but she left me for my proctologist. Turns out he really knew how to look deep into things.

dialogue.speaker: Tippy
dialogue.line: I tried to sue them both, but my lawyer hypnotized me into thinking I had no case. Now I'm financially ruined—turns out hypnosis really is a mind game.

jokes.text: 'You'll pay for this one day.' — Moira threatening... the television? A character on screen?
jokes.setup: Moira is watching and reacting to Sunrise Bay drama.
jokes.punchline: It's unclear if she's reacting to the show's plot or making a real-life threat — the ambiguity is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Moira's dramatic pronouncements bleeding between fiction and reality is a recurring device; this instance is concise and ambiguous enough to work.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You look wonderful, Clifton. Have you been taking care of yourself?

dialogue.speaker: Clifton
dialogue.line: Well, I wouldn't say that. I mean, I'm sober.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: That's wonderful!

dialogue.speaker: Clifton
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, I haven't had a drink since yesterday at noon.

jokes.text: The visual of Johnny and Stevie eating the wedding tasting food in a back room — Stevie caught still eating the tenderloin.
jokes.setup: The wedding tasting is presented as a grand, stressful affair for John.
jokes.punchline: Stevie, as Maid of Honour 'just here to eat,' is later found privately still eating the tenderloin.
jokes.explanation: Good character-consistent visual. Stevie's unabashed mercenary attendance is funny.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Clifton, your performance was absolutely transcendent. The vulnerability, the raw emotion—you've truly captured the complexity of familial discord.

dialogue.speaker: Clifton
dialogue.line: Thank you, but I should clarify—that wasn't acting.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm sorry?

dialogue.speaker: Clifton
dialogue.line: That was just my actual family. We're really like that.

jokes.text: David arrives two minutes late and declares the wedding doors would have been closed
jokes.setup: David arrives slightly late to the wedding tasting
jokes.punchline: He immediately turns his own lateness into a lesson about the rules, punishing himself theoretically while being the offender
jokes.explanation: David's self-righteousness applied to himself is a nice character moment. The fact that he's lecturing about punctuality while being the late one is classic David cognitive dissonance.

dialogue.speaker: Clifton
dialogue.line: I haven't actually seen it.

jokes.text: Johnny takes and eats what turns out to be the garnish, remarking it's 'a little side salad' — 'Poor thing. That's the garnish for the lobster roll.'
jokes.setup: Johnny is trying to show engagement with the food while also watching the prices.
jokes.punchline: He enthusiastically eats garnish as a course, mistaking presentation for food.
jokes.explanation: Classic fish-out-of-water comedy. Johnny's budget anxiety makes him overcompensate into ignorance. 'Poor thing' from David is a nice reaction beat.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, so how did she die?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I was pushed off a balcony by my evil twin, fell into a vat of acid, got shot by a sniper, and then hit by a bus.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: All of that?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: They wanted to make sure I was really dead.

jokes.text: 'Are we inviting The Vanderbilts?' — Johnny's alarmed reaction to the lobster roll.
jokes.setup: David has ordered lobster rolls for the wedding menu.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's incredulous Vanderbilt reference — a man who used to charter jets for weddings — now finding lobster extravagant.
jokes.explanation: Excellent character joke because of the dramatic fall it implies. This man who used to actually hobnob with the Vanderbilt tier is now shocked by a lobster roll. The reference does a lot of biographical work. High character integration justified.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I mean, it was quite the artistic choice. The vulnerability of the nude form, combined with the theatrical staging of the demise... it was almost Shakespearean in its tragedy.

jokes.text: 'if we're not using the lettuce under the lobster roll can we just not make a side salad out of this lettuce?' — Johnny's budget-saving suggestion.
jokes.setup: The caterer has suggested a premium parmesan and black truffle salad; David enthusiastically agrees.
jokes.punchline: Johnny proposes recycling the garnish as the salad course — peak frugality that would horrify any wedding caterer.
jokes.explanation: The callback to the garnish mistake gives this extra punch — now he's not just eating garnish, he's proposing to serve garnish as a course at his son's wedding. The line is long but every word earns its place. This perfectly captures Johnny's arc from extravagance to desperate frugality.

dialogue.speaker: Clifton Powell
dialogue.line: Yeah, I may have had something to do with her departure from the show. Let's just say I made some strategic "career decisions" for her.

jokes.text: 'Are these prices per table?' / 'Um, per plate.' — John's horrified clarification.
jokes.setup: Johnny is anxiously watching costs at the tasting.
jokes.punchline: The pause before Penelope's 'per plate' and Johnny's implied devastation.
jokes.explanation: A reliable joke type (cost sticker shock) executed adequately. The beat works but it's a familiar gag.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I don't know what all the fuss is about. I've popped a breasts myself.

jokes.text: 'And speaking of, we are adding eight more plates. I was finally allowed to invite some friends from New York, they're a caviar crowd.'
jokes.setup: Johnny is already alarmed by the per-plate pricing.
jokes.punchline: David immediately adds eight more plates and describes the guests as 'a caviar crowd' — implying even higher expectations.
jokes.explanation: Good escalation — just as Johnny starts processing the base cost, David piles on. 'A caviar crowd' is a funny, specific detail that implies David still lives mentally in his old world.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I've been doing some research online about how to be a better homemaker.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I'm sure you'll be clicking all the right buttons. Though I suspect your mouse work could use some improving.

jokes.text: Johnny tastes the beef tenderloin and offers a pained 'it's fine,' then immediately pivots to 'it's the best thing I've ever tasted.'
jokes.setup: Johnny tries to steer David away from expensive beef tenderloin by pretending it's mediocre.
jokes.punchline: He can't sustain the lie for even five seconds — 'it's fine' shatters instantly into rapturous honesty.
jokes.explanation: Well-structured misdirection. The setup is Johnny's obvious strategy; the punchline is his immediate, complete failure at it. The rapid collapse from strained 'it's fine' to 'best thing I've ever tasted' is great physical/verbal comedy. Character-specific: Johnny Rose cannot lie about food.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Dad, you can't just... you can't treat a gourmet food tasting like you're browsing in a gift shop.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: What do you mean? I'm tasting things.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You just said 'ooh, that's nice' and put it down. That's not tasting, that's shopping.

jokes.text: 'Well, you don't want people coming to your wedding and only talking about the food.' / 'That is literally the only thing I want them talking about. What is wrong with you?'
jokes.setup: Johnny tries to spin the amazing food as a reason NOT to serve it at the wedding.
jokes.punchline: David's response reveals the disconnect: for Johnny food is a distraction; for David it IS the point.
jokes.explanation: A perfect two-hander. Johnny's folksy wisdom is completely wrong-headed and David's exasperated correction ('What is wrong with you?') lands hard. The joke reveals both characters' values simultaneously.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: They gave us extra money because Patrick's last engagement fell through, and they feel terrible about it. So, in a way, I'm benefiting from his ex-fiancée's heartbreak. Which is... fine. I'm comfortable with that.

jokes.text: 'Why not, it's not like we're paying for the samples.' / [pause] 'We're not paying for the samples, are we?'
jokes.setup: Johnny encourages himself to try the duck confit because samples are free.
jokes.punchline: He immediately second-guesses this assumption in front of the caterer — the panic arrives mid-bite.
jokes.explanation: Excellent timing joke. The self-permission followed immediately by self-doubt is a perfect comedic rhythm. The structure — declaration, pause, horrified question — is classic and executed crisply. When Penelope confirms samples are free, his immediate request for 'a bit more tenderloin' is the cherry on top.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We were going to have ice sculptures of ourselves at the wedding. Life-sized. In ice. Now we're lucky if we can afford a sheet cake from the grocery store that nobody's allergic to.

jokes.text: 'Oh. Then do you wanna put a bit more tenderloin on there, please.' — Johnny's shameless request once samples are confirmed free.
jokes.setup: Johnny has been anxious about costs all through the tasting.
jokes.punchline: The instant the samples are confirmed free, he shamelessly requests more of the most expensive item.
jokes.explanation: The payoff to both the samples anxiety and the suppressed tenderloin love. Johnny's character clarity is perfect here — he wants to save money AND get the best food. Confirmed free? Load up. Good callback.

jokes.text: 'Moira Fucking Rose.' — Tippy Bernstein's awestruck greeting.
jokes.setup: Moira enters the café for her power lunch meeting.
jokes.punchline: Tippy's explosive greeting — the middle name is not Rose's middle name but his exclamation — is both tribute and absurdist character establishment.
jokes.explanation: A great introduction for a new character. Establishes Tippy as theatrical, industry-insider, and genuinely wowed by Moira. The profanity as superlative is a well-worn device but executed with gusto.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: My character was trampled to death by a herd of bulls during the running of the bulls in Pamplona. It was very sudden. One moment she was fleeing for her life in a white linen dress, and the next, she was gored through the abdomen by a particularly aggressive bull with what I can only describe as a personal vendetta against her.

jokes.text: 'You are just as gorgeous as the day I discovered you.' / 'Oh Tippy. You're as alive as ever!'
jokes.setup: Tippy pays Moira a flattering compliment.
jokes.punchline: Moira's return compliment is 'You're as alive as ever' — which is simultaneously a compliment and a low bar. The subtext (he looks terrible but is still breathing) is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Pure Moira — she can't quite bring herself to call him gorgeous so she settles for 'alive.' The politeness-as-shade device is perfectly executed. High character integration: only Moira would say this.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You sabotaged my career!

dialogue.speaker: Clifton
dialogue.line: I was trying to protect you. You're too talented for this business.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: That's not protection, that's sabotage!

dialogue.speaker: Clifton
dialogue.line: Well, I suppose it's a backhanded compliment then.

jokes.text: Tippy's vertigo cure story: 'Cured that vertigo years ago. I hired a hypnotherapist. Then I married her. And then I divorced her. You-are-getting-very... broke.'
jokes.setup: Tippy explains he cured his vertigo by hiring a hypnotherapist.
jokes.punchline: The escalating life events (hired, married, divorced) culminate in Tippy's punch line doing a hypnotist bit: 'You are getting very... broke.'
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes in the episode. The structure is near-perfect: the story escalates through three life stages (employee, wife, ex-wife) and lands on a hypnotist punchline that reframes all of it as a joke about the divorce costing him money. The callback to hypnosis as the delivery mechanism for the punchline is structurally elegant. Highly quotable, completely original in its specific execution, and the character of Tippy is fully established in one joke. Comparable to the 8.0 anchor or above.

dialogue.speaker: Tippy
dialogue.line: I've submitted you for every award every year.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, that's very sweet of you, Tippy.

dialogue.speaker: Tippy
dialogue.line: Even for that role where you played your own brother on Days of Our Lives.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: That was a very complicated story arc.

jokes.text: 'Tippy, I have missed that vaudevillian charm.'
jokes.setup: Tippy has just delivered his hypnotherapist joke.
jokes.punchline: 'Vaudevillian charm' is a polite way of saying old-fashioned and slightly corny — Moira appreciates it while subtly insulting it.
jokes.explanation: Moira's vocabulary as comedy. 'Vaudevillian' is precisely the word she'd use. It flatters and slightly condescends in equal measure.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, I got a signed photo of John Stamos.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: It says 'To Roland, keep it real.' He signed it 'John Stamos.'

jokes.text: Clifton Sparks enters and greets Moira as 'Muffy.'
jokes.setup: After Moira has been called 'Moira Fucking Rose,' a grand figure.
jokes.punchline: Clifton's nickname 'Muffy' completely deflates the mystique — implying a more embarrassing, familiar version of Moira that predates her star persona.
jokes.explanation: Good deflation joke. 'Muffy' undercuts everything that came before and gives Clifton an instant character beat.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You know what your problem is? You're thinking too small. You gotta create more problems. More problems means more solutions, and more solutions means more money.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: That's not... that's not how business works, Roland.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, it's worked for me for thirty years.

jokes.text: 'Oh, had I known you were both coming, I'd have dressed for the occasion.' — Moira, fully done up.
jokes.setup: Clifton arrives unexpectedly alongside Tippy.
jokes.punchline: Moira is clearly dressed to the nines; claiming she'd have dressed up is pure theatrical false modesty.
jokes.explanation: Classic Moira false modesty — she's always 'dressed for the occasion' and everyone knows it. Solid character joke.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You know what Wayne Gretzky says? You miss 100% of the tapes you don't take.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I think you mean shots.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: No, no, tapes. VHS tapes. That's what I built my business on.

jokes.text: 'Gave up the drink. Strictly a wine and scotch man now.' — Clifton's sobriety claim.
jokes.setup: Moira compliments Clifton on looking 'fresh and dewy'; he attributes it to sobriety.
jokes.punchline: He immediately undermines his sobriety by listing wine and scotch as things he still drinks.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed self-contradiction. The joke is in the absolute confidence with which Clifton delivers his own paradox — he has no idea he's been funny. Highly quotable and very economical.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I've been reading your book, Johnny. 'To my family, who believed in me when I didn't believe in myself.' Very touching.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Thank you, Roland. I'm glad you're enjoying it.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: So when does the murder happen? I'm on chapter two and nobody's been killed yet. I thought this was a thriller.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, it's a memoir.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: A memoir? Then why does the dedication make it sound like you're about to solve a crime?

jokes.text: 'Cliffy! Cliffy, I have not seen you since you starred in that electrifying courtroom drama about the potty mouth, feral child.' / 'Oh, that was an actual court case. My daughter wanted to emancipate and the press couldn't get enough.'
jokes.setup: Moira remembers Clifton's 'electrifying courtroom drama about the potty mouth feral child.'
jokes.punchline: It wasn't a movie — it was his real daughter trying to legally escape him, covered live by press.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional joke. The misdirection is perfect: 'courtroom drama about the potty mouth, feral child' sounds like a soap opera plot but is his actual child. The detail that 'the press couldn't get enough' and the breezy way Clifton recounts it makes him instantly hilarious as a character. Comparable to the 8.0 anchor and arguably above it. High marks across all categories.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I want script approval, final say on wardrobe, and a dedicated parking spot.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: And they need to apologize publicly for that incident with the craft services.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Every cast member must address me by my full name before 9 AM.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: And they have to rename the soundstage after me.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Mom, that's insane.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: And I want a car. A new car. Actually, multiple cars. A fleet.

jokes.text: 'Lucky you!' — Moira's response to hearing Clifton's daughter tried to legally emancipate from him.
jokes.setup: Clifton has just described his daughter's legal emancipation attempt.
jokes.punchline: Moira's cheerful 'Lucky you!' — congratulating him on generating press from his family trauma.
jokes.explanation: Two words doing maximal comedic work. 'Lucky you!' is an extraordinary response to learning a child tried to legally escape their parent — Moira reduces it entirely to a PR win. Perfect character integration (only Moira), perfect economy (two words), dark and funny. The cheerfulness of delivery makes it land harder.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Are you doing the reboot?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I made them an offer they couldn't refuse. I asked for final cut, approval of all creative decisions, and my own private dressing room with a bidet that plays classical music.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Mom, that's insane.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Exactly. I'm not interested in reboots, darling. I'm interested in never being asked about reboots again.

jokes.text: 'I can't wait for the screener.' — Clifton's response when asked what he thought of Moira's Crows movie.
jokes.setup: Moira mentions her 'Crow picture' and asks Clifton's opinion.
jokes.punchline: He hasn't seen it — a perfectly industry-insider response that implies he's been meaning to but hasn't gotten around to supporting her work.
jokes.explanation: Sharp and specific to the entertainment industry — everyone says they're 'waiting for the screener.' The delivery as if it's a compliment is perfectly calibrated. High economy.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, why is she not acknowledging that he's literally right there above her?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's a soap opera, Alexis. Blocking isn't exactly their strong suit.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: But he's like, directly in her line of sight. How do you not see that?

jokes.text: 'They wanna reboot Sunrise Bay!' / '...And I was believing you both up till now.' — Moira's incredulous reaction to Nicole Kidman being attached.
jokes.setup: Tippy and Clifton progressively reveal an exciting reboot opportunity, culminating in Nicole Kidman.
jokes.punchline: The absurdity of the escalating good news causes Moira to assume it's a lie — the best news is too good to believe.
jokes.explanation: Good escalation payoff. Moira's disbelief at Nicole Kidman is a nice beat — and her 'I was believing you both up till now' is a funny, measured way to express it.

jokes.text: Tippy produces the contract offer: 'Oops, oops. What do we have here? What is that? Is this maybe a very generous offer for a direct to-series primetime Emmy contender.'
jokes.setup: Moira is skeptical; Tippy needs to close the deal.
jokes.punchline: Tippy's theatrical 'discovery' of the contract he clearly had ready — the worst pretense of improvisation — is a character-perfect moment for a showbiz veteran.
jokes.explanation: The performance of discovering something he obviously brought is a good Tippy character moment. A bit long in the delivery but the showbiz phoniness is well-captured.

jokes.text: 'It's a simple answer, Vivian. Say yes.' — Clifton calls Moira by her character name.
jokes.setup: Moira is hesitating over the offer.
jokes.punchline: Clifton slides into calling her by her character's name — whether as a manipulation or habit — is a funny blurring of actor and role.
jokes.explanation: Nice subtle meta moment. The blurring of Vivian and Moira is the episode's theme, and this small beat captures it.

jokes.text: Alexis's comprehensive recap of Moira's character's death: 'you vomited a demon into a toilet, and then fell in and drowned.'
jokes.setup: Moira claims her death wasn't humiliating.
jokes.punchline: Alexis's detailed, matter-of-fact recap of demon vomiting and toilet drowning — delivered as if it's merely factual — is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Wonderfully specific soap opera parody. The specificity of 'vomited a demon into a toilet, and then fell in and drowned' is the gift — each element is funnier than the last. The matter-of-fact delivery (inferred from Alexis's character) is perfect. Strong originality in the detail selection.

jokes.text: 'Well, the writers insisted it was symbolic.' — Moira defending the demon toilet death.
jokes.setup: Alexis has just described the undeniably ridiculous circumstances of Moira's character death.
jokes.punchline: Moira defends it earnestly as 'symbolic' — the actor who has convinced herself the soap opera death was Art.
jokes.explanation: Perfect Moira self-delusion. Only Moira Rose would claim the demon toilet death was symbolic and say it with complete conviction. High character integration and economy. The contrast between the absurd death and Moira's dignity in describing it is the joke.

jokes.text: 'I should have seen that coming after the contract demands' — Moira briefly acknowledging she possibly negotiated herself off the show.
jokes.setup: The conspiracy theory that Clifton had Moira written off.
jokes.punchline: Moira half-admits her own role in a completely matter-of-fact aside.
jokes.explanation: Small but telling character beat. Moira's self-awareness appears in flashes and this is a good one.

jokes.text: Alexis reveals she fell 'down a dark Sunrise fan-hole' and found conspiracy theories on message boards
jokes.setup: Alexis has been watching all day and researching
jokes.punchline: The image of Alexis deep in soap opera fan conspiracy theory forums — the juxtaposition of her glamorous exterior with internet rabbit-holing
jokes.explanation: Functional character comedy — Alexis going down the fan theory rabbit hole is consistent and mildly funny but doesn't have a particularly sharp punchline.

jokes.text: The fan forum conspiracy video: Clifton saying 'She had a good run. But let's just say "someone" reminded the producers that there's only room for one head of surgery at Sunrise General. And I think all you housewives at home know who that should be.'
jokes.setup: Alexis has found a video where Clifton apparently confesses to having Moira written off the show.
jokes.punchline: The footage is so incriminating that the air quotes, the 'housewives' dig, and the self-congratulation make Clifton look guilty without actually admitting anything — and the soap opera logic (head of surgery) bleeds into reality.
jokes.explanation: The joke has layers: Clifton using the fictional hierarchy ('head of surgery') to describe real showbiz power plays is funny, and the 'housewives' patronizing dig is a perfect period-piece detail. Good structural writing.

jokes.text: 'See, he's basically admitting to having you written off the show.' / 'No. He's drunk there. No. That's not... No.'
jokes.setup: The video appears damning.
jokes.punchline: Moira's first instinct is total denial ('He's drunk there') — she needs to believe her own narrative rather than confront the betrayal.
jokes.explanation: Moira's denial reaction is character-consistent and the rapid-fire 'No's are good rhythm comedy.

jokes.text: 'Adriana had that DUI last year. Popped a breast, poor dear.' — Moira dismissing competition for headlines.
jokes.setup: Alexis says Moira is the only one making headlines in the cast.
jokes.punchline: Moira concedes Adriana made news but only due to a DUI and a wardrobe malfunction — and delivers 'poor dear' with zero actual sympathy.
jokes.explanation: 'Popped a breast, poor dear' is a fantastic line — the specificity, the casual dismissal, and the fake sympathy of 'poor dear' (classic Moira) all land. The economy is excellent and it's very quotable.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Alexis, he drove all this way just to personally beg me to acquiesce.' / Alexis: 'Hm. Of course he did.'
jokes.setup: Moira frames Clifton's presence as desperate begging
jokes.punchline: Alexis's 'of course he did' — she now knows it's because Clifton sabotaged Moira and needs her back, but Moira reads it as straightforward admiration of her star power
jokes.explanation: A nice dramatic irony joke — Alexis knows why he's really there (guilt/manipulation) while Moira thinks it's pure admiration. The two characters interpret 'of course he did' completely differently.

jokes.text: 'Perhaps a little more housework and a little less mouse work might do you some good, Alexis.'
jokes.setup: Alexis has been doing online research on the fan forum.
jokes.punchline: Moira's malapropism: 'mouse work' for internet research (computer mouse) combined with 'housework' — delivered as if it's a coherent piece of wisdom.
jokes.explanation: Moira's invented compound idioms are one of the show's signatures and 'mouse work' is a great addition to the lexicon. The dismissal of online research as 'mouse work' via a pun on computer mouse is both original and completely character-specific. 'Housework and mouse work' has a satisfying sonic quality too.

jokes.text: Good news about Phil the fumigator: 'He's got a lot of two-star reviews, but that's one more star than the other guys.'
jokes.setup: Roland reports good news about a cheaper fumigator.
jokes.punchline: The 'good news' is a two-star professional being the BEST option available.
jokes.explanation: Good Roland logic. The framing of two stars as a positive ('one more than the other guys') is funny and perfectly Roland — optimism built on catastrophically low standards.

jokes.text: 'Well, they were just gonna throw it out, David.' — Johnny explaining why he's still eating wedding tasting tenderloin.
jokes.setup: David finds Johnny still eating tenderloin after the tasting.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's justification — 'they were going to throw it out' — is the universal excuse for eating something one shouldn't.
jokes.explanation: Good callback payoff — after all the tasting comedy, Johnny is still eating the tenderloin. The excuse is universal and delivered with appropriate sheepishness.

jokes.text: Penelope asked if Johnny wanted to crack the crème brûlée and he asked 'If we break it, do we buy it?'
jokes.setup: David is trying to understand what happened to make Johnny uncomfortable at the tasting.
jokes.punchline: The offscreen crème brûlée incident — Johnny applying pottery barn rules to dessert — is funnier for being revealed secondhand.
jokes.explanation: Excellent offscreen callback. 'If we break it, do we buy it?' applied to crème brûlée is a perfect encapsulation of Johnny's mindset — shopkeeper anxiety applied to a dessert course. Revealed in David's retelling makes it land even harder.

jokes.text: 'Well, I think that was a perfectly legitimate question considering I wasn't even hungry after the beef.'
jokes.setup: David points out the crème brûlée question was embarrassing.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's defense — using his excessive beef eating as an alibi — only highlights how much he ate for free.
jokes.explanation: Good defensive rationalization joke. The beef alibi inadvertently reveals how much free food Johnny consumed.

jokes.text: David reveals Patrick's ex-fiancée's nest egg will cover the extra table: 'Apparently there is some nest egg they set aside for Patrick and his ex-fiancée.'
jokes.setup: David is reassuring Johnny he doesn't have to cover the extra table
jokes.punchline: The source of funds is money saved for Patrick's previous engagement — a slightly dark comedic irony that the broken engagement is funding the current one
jokes.explanation: A nicely dark irony — the ex-fiancée's misfortune (the broken engagement) becomes the financial windfall for the current wedding. David delivering this without apparent awareness of the awkwardness is mildly funny.

jokes.text: 'David, I said I would cover the food... Okay. But if the Brewers wanna pay for the extra table, then I think that's a wonderful gesture from the Brewers.'
jokes.setup: David offers to remove the cost pressure by noting Patrick's parents will cover the extra table.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's grateful, dignified pivot — first insisting, then immediately accepting — is a perfect comedy beat of paternal pride yielding to relief.
jokes.explanation: The beat between the pride and the acceptance is the joke — and then 'a wonderful gesture from the Brewers' is a perfectly diplomatic way to accept charity.

jokes.text: 'Great. What did I tell you about that beef?' — Johnny immediately pivoting to the tenderloin after his money stress is relieved.
jokes.setup: The financial pressure has just lifted slightly.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's first reaction is to revisit the tenderloin — revealing food was the real emotional concern all along.
jokes.explanation: Good tag on the tasting sequence. The immediate return to tenderloin appreciation shows Johnny's priorities in a funny light.

jokes.text: Johnny's emotional confessional to Stevie about their former wealth: 'He was talking about Bali for a while. We were gonna charter some jets. Fly everybody.'
jokes.setup: Johnny shares that they had a nest egg set aside for David's wedding.
jokes.punchline: The specific, vivid details of the old life — Bali, chartered jets — that are now completely out of reach make the gap between then and now funny and sad simultaneously.
jokes.explanation: Warm, bittersweet comedy. The specifics (chartered jets to Bali for 'everybody') paint a vivid picture of the Rose family's former excess. The comedy is in the contrast, which is the show's fundamental engine.

jokes.text: 'Tell that cute bartender I'd love a sex on the beach. If she has time, I need another scotch.'
jokes.setup: The group is celebrating at a bar; Tippy addresses drink orders.
jokes.punchline: Tippy manages to make a pass at the bartender and order his actual drink in the same breath, prioritizing the flirtation as primary and the scotch as an afterthought.
jokes.explanation: Fun Tippy character beat. The ordering priority (flirtation > scotch) tells us who he is efficiently.

jokes.text: 'Oh no, this doctor would rather nurse her potable, if you don't mind. Best to stay lucid for our congress.'
jokes.setup: Tippy encourages Moira to drink.
jokes.punchline: Moira uses 'potable' (liquid/drink) and 'congress' (meeting) in their most formal, archaic senses — playing 'the doctor' of their fictional show in real life while making even drinking sound like a diplomatic summit.
jokes.explanation: Moiraism — 'nurse her potable' and 'our congress' are exactly the words Moira Rose would use. The character is performing the role even in casual conversation.

jokes.text: 'Hey. Remember how you entered that time machine you discovered in the hospital's broom closet? Season Three? What if we bring you back that way?'
jokes.setup: The group is trying to figure out how to resurrect Moira's character for the reboot.
jokes.punchline: A broom closet time machine in a hospital is presented as a viable, precedented plot mechanism — said casually as a normal industry consideration.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'broom closet' time machine is great soap opera parody. That they're casually workshopping this as if it's a reasonable logistical solution is the comedic engine.

jokes.text: 'It's not easy to disremember my final demise.'
jokes.setup: Tippy asks Moira to move past how her character was killed.
jokes.punchline: 'Disremember' — Moira's invented word that conflates 'disregard' and 'remember' — used with total confidence.
jokes.explanation: 'Disremember' is a beautiful Moiraism — a word that shouldn't exist but somehow makes sense in context, delivered with utter conviction. This is one of the defining character comedy devices of the show. High character integration (9.5) is absolutely justified — this is one of those words that belongs to Moira and only Moira.

jokes.text: Clifton describing Vivian's death: 'It was even more vicious than Vivian's getting trampled while honeymooning in Pamplona.'
jokes.setup: The group is comparing Vivian's death to Clifton's character's death.
jokes.punchline: Vivian being trampled while honeymooning in Pamplona (the running of the bulls, implied) is presented as the benchmark for vicious deaths — and Moira's demon toilet death is apparently worse.
jokes.explanation: Excellent soap opera absurdism. The Pamplona honeymoon trampling is a wonderfully specific soap opera death, and using it as the baseline that the demon toilet death apparently surpassed is funny escalation.

jokes.text: 'Oh Moira! Get over it! Contract negotiations go sour all the time!'
jokes.setup: Moira is upset about how she was written off the show.
jokes.punchline: Clifton's blunt, self-incriminating dismissal of Moira's complaint — especially given what we suspect he did — is darkly funny.
jokes.explanation: The dramatic irony makes this land — we suspect Clifton sabotaged her, so his 'get over it' is doubly funny.

jokes.text: 'Think of it as a compliment. Your star was rising, and I was maybe jealous.'
jokes.setup: Clifton confirms he was partly responsible for Moira being written off.
jokes.punchline: 'Think of it as a compliment' — reframing career sabotage as admiration is an audacious, funny spin.
jokes.explanation: A great line. The brass of reframing sabotage as tribute, delivered with apparent sincerity, is very funny and very specific to Hollywood sociopathy.

jokes.text: Tippy is getting texts from Nicole Kidman's office: 'Serves me right for sharing EP credit. These people...'
jokes.setup: Clifton and Moira are having an intense moment of reconciliation.
jokes.punchline: Tippy derails the emotional gravity with a casual, industry-insider complaint about Nicole Kidman's management.
jokes.explanation: Good tonal contrast — the gravitas of Clifton's confession is interrupted by showbiz triviality. Tippy as the mercenary producer unaware of emotional weight is a good character beat.

jokes.text: 'That season that you played your own brother, we submitted you for Best Actress and Actor.'
jokes.setup: Tippy is defending his track record of supporting Moira's awards submissions.
jokes.punchline: The casual reveal that Moira played her own brother at some point — and was submitted for both gendered acting categories — is a wonderfully absurd soap opera detail.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional soap opera absurdism. The dual submission for playing her own brother is an inspired piece of background worldbuilding. The deadpan delivery of 'Best Actress and Actor' implies the logic was completely sound to everyone involved. Highly quotable and a great laugh.

jokes.text: 'You did what?' / 'Oh, Tippy, I did not negotiate myself out of the job, did I?'
jokes.setup: Moira hears she was submitted for both acting categories simultaneously.
jokes.punchline: The revelation that Tippy submitted her for both awards — which only happens if you play a character of another gender — triggers Moira's realization that maybe she was also wrong about being driven off the show.
jokes.explanation: Good structural payoff. The moment connects the dual submission to Moira's self-examination. Well-played narrative comedy.

jokes.text: Moira at the bar: 'Now, Mr. Bernstein, I have a few demands.'
jokes.setup: Moira has decided to negotiate rather than simply accept.
jokes.punchline: The formality of 'Mr. Bernstein' combined with 'a few demands' — the shift to power negotiation — is pure Moira.
jokes.explanation: Good transition moment. The pivot to power mode is character-consistent and funny.

jokes.text: 'I'm willing to sell my baseball signed by Geena Davis.'
jokes.setup: Roland is brainstorming how to raise money for the motel.
jokes.punchline: A baseball signed by Geena Davis is Roland's most prized asset — and the specificity of Geena Davis (not a sports figure, an actress known for baseball movie A League of Their Own) is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist detail. A baseball signed by Geena Davis is a wonderfully specific piece of Roland's world — the crossover of athlete signage with an actress who played a baseball player is either ignorance or extremely niche fandom, both of which are funny. It tells you everything about Roland.

jokes.text: 'And by "situation" he means that money-pit of a motel we bought.'
jokes.setup: Johnny uses the diplomatic word 'situation' to describe their financial crisis.
jokes.punchline: Roland translates it bluntly, with the added specificity of 'money-pit.'
jokes.explanation: Functional Roland-as-blunt-translator joke. Gets the job done efficiently.

jokes.text: 'What if we bought 30 more motels? Right now.'
jokes.setup: The team is stuck between selling the second motel or finding another way out.
jokes.punchline: Stevie's counterintuitive, seemingly insane escalation — solving a financial crisis by buying 30 more things — delivered with complete deadpan.
jokes.explanation: Excellent Stevie deadpan. The addition of 'Right now' is the comedic masterstroke — the urgency applied to the ridiculous is perfect. The idea is absurd but actually correct (as the scene reveals), making it even funnier in retrospect.

jokes.text: 'Stevie, where did you come up with this?' / 'Oh, it was in a book I was given. I don't know, maybe you read it.'
jokes.setup: Johnny is impressed by Stevie's business expansion plan.
jokes.punchline: Stevie reveals it came from the book Johnny gave to all the new staff at orientation — meaning the plan came from Johnny's own playbook, which he apparently didn't review closely himself.
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback with a structural payoff. The book distributed at the orientation becomes the source of the solution, and 'maybe you read it' is a perfect dry dig — implying Johnny didn't study his own material. The structure is tight: setup at 01:55, pay off here. High scores across the board are justified.

jokes.text: 'A wise man once wrote: "You miss 100% of the tapes you don't play".'
jokes.setup: Johnny is inspired by the expansion plan and wants to build momentum.
jokes.punchline: Roland quotes from Johnny's book, but the Wayne Gretzky hockey quote is mangled through VHS tape metaphors — consistent with Roland's earlier confusion about the book.
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes in the episode. 'You miss 100% of the tapes you don't play' is a masterful rewriting of the Wayne Gretzky quote filtered through VHS tape confusion — which callbacks Roland's earlier 'why is he making us read about VHS tapes' joke. The quote now sounds like genuine wisdom while being completely wrong. It's quotable, structurally elegant, and a perfect payoff to both the book setup and Roland's confusion. High scores justified.

jokes.text: 'I did write that.' — Johnny accepting credit for the mangled quote.
jokes.setup: Roland has quoted 'You miss 100% of the tapes you don't play' as Johnny's wisdom.
jokes.punchline: Johnny claims authorship of what is obviously a corrupted Gretzky quote — either playing along or genuinely believing it.
jokes.explanation: Perfect tag. Three words, maximal implication. Johnny's instant ownership of the corrupted wisdom is either magnificent confidence or complete unawareness — both are funny.

jokes.text: 'And I know that a wise man once wrote: "Thank you to my wife, Moira."' / 'That's just the dedication, Roland.'
jokes.setup: Roland continues to quote from the book.
jokes.punchline: Roland has only read the dedication — confirming he never got past the first page.
jokes.explanation: Excellent structural payoff. The escalating 'wise man' structure (quote, then dedication) beautifully reveals that Roland's entire engagement with the book was the dedication. 'That's just the dedication, Roland' is a perfectly dry Johnny line. The joke has great structure and character integration — Roland would absolutely quote the dedication as wisdom.

jokes.text: 'That's as far as I got, Johnny. I'm sorry, I'm partial to historical fiction.'
jokes.setup: Johnny points out that Roland has only read the dedication.
jokes.punchline: Roland's completely unembarrassed admission, further explained by his preference for historical fiction — as if that's a reasonable excuse for not reading a business book given to him.
jokes.explanation: Great tag. Roland's complete lack of shame combined with the specific and irrelevant explanation ('I'm partial to historical fiction') is perfect character comedy. The non-sequitur literary preference makes it funnier.

jokes.text: 'Oh Alexis, has our quarantine been lifted?' — Moira greeting Alexis who emerges from bed.
jokes.setup: Moira told Alexis to stay hidden; Alexis has now emerged.
jokes.punchline: Describing Alexis's bed rest as a 'quarantine' — treating her appearance as a public health event.
jokes.explanation: Good callback. The 'quarantine' framing is a nice escalation of Moira's 'where no one can see you' earlier.

jokes.text: 'Yeah, I was scared I was gonna get bed sores, so I'm going for a run.'
jokes.setup: Alexis explains why she's emerged from her room.
jokes.punchline: The extreme (bed sores from a few hours' rest) stated as a real medical concern is funny, especially delivered casually.
jokes.explanation: Mild but functional. Alexis's health paranoia (bed sores from a nap) is character-consistent and delivered with appropriate casualness.

jokes.text: 'eight hours of watching "Sunrise Bay" made me feel a little not-right in the head.' / 'It had that effect. The New England Journal of Medicine did a fascinating study on it.'
jokes.setup: Alexis reports feeling off after binge-watching the soap opera.
jokes.punchline: Moira confirms Sunrise Bay is medically documented to cause neurological effects — said completely straight.
jokes.explanation: Excellent Moira line. The New England Journal of Medicine studying Sunrise Bay's effect on viewers is a wonderfully absurd detail, delivered with complete seriousness. It's both self-aggrandizing (her show was notable enough to be studied) and self-aware (it made people feel 'not-right').

jokes.text: 'Will you be trapped in this crystal the entire episode? Best to skip ahead.'
jokes.setup: Alexis sees Moira's character trapped in a crystal on the TV screen.
jokes.punchline: Moira casually advises skipping ahead through her own storyline — either because it's boring or because she remembers it's a bad stretch.
jokes.explanation: Wonderfully meta. Moira directing the viewing experience of her own old work ('best to skip ahead') is funny and adds to the soap opera parody. The 'trapped in a crystal' detail is inspired absurdist worldbuilding.

jokes.text: 'But on the condition that Clifton be written off the show — death of my choosing.'
jokes.setup: Moira reveals she agreed to do the reboot.
jokes.punchline: Moira's condition is revenge by proxy — she's not just accepting the deal, she's engineering Clifton's fictional death, and the 'death of my choosing' is perfectly dramatic.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's standout moments. The revenge condition is petty, specific, and utterly Moira — 'death of my choosing' has the ring of a queen sentencing someone. It's funny, character-defining, and deeply satisfying dramatically. High scores across the board are justified.

jokes.text: 'And I asked for more money.' / 'Okay. Yass, Mom.' / 'And a diamond tennis bracelet.' / 'Okay. Well, that seems like a bit much.'
jokes.setup: Moira lists her demands: death for Clifton, more money.
jokes.punchline: Alexis is on board for the first two, but the diamond tennis bracelet is a bridge too far — the escalation to pure luxury demand as a non-negotiable breaks even Alexis.
jokes.explanation: Good escalation. Alexis's 'Yass, Mom' is great — she endorses Clifton's death and a pay raise but balks at the bracelet. The joke is in what crosses the line for Alexis (not the revenge killing, but the jewelry).

jokes.text: 'Oh, God no. I made them an offer they had to refuse.' / 'So, you're not doing it then?'
jokes.setup: Alexis asks if Moira got 'what she's worth.'
jokes.punchline: Moira's response inverts the Godfather line ('offer they couldn't refuse') to mean she deliberately asked for too much — a deliberate sabotage of the deal.
jokes.explanation: Excellent structural joke. The Godfather inversion ('offer they had to refuse') is a clean, elegant piece of wordplay that also reveals character — Moira is protecting herself from a world that hurt her, using her own theatrical language to do it. It earns its impact score. One of the most quotable lines of the episode.

jokes.text: 'No. Thank you, Alexis. Thank you for removing my rosy lunettes, so that I might see Clifton for who he really is.'
jokes.setup: Moira explains why she turned down the offer.
jokes.punchline: 'Rosy lunettes' — rose-colored glasses, literally translated into a Moira-ism combining her surname ('Rose') with the French word for glasses.
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Moiraism. 'Rosy lunettes' is the best invented expression in the episode — it translates 'rose-colored glasses' but: (1) uses the French 'lunettes' for glasses, (2) 'rosy' connects to her actual surname, (3) it's delivered as if it's a real idiom. The density of meaning per syllable is exceptional. High character integration is fully warranted — this could only be Moira.

jokes.text: 'If you did all that without getting out of bed, imagine what you're capable of doing.'
jokes.setup: Moira is complimenting Alexis's sleuthing work.
jokes.punchline: The backhanded nature of the compliment — 'without getting out of bed' implies the standard of getting out of bed is the real achievement threshold for Alexis.
jokes.explanation: Warm but subtly cutting Moira compliment. The subtext (the bar for Alexis is merely being ambulatory) is the joke.

jokes.text: 'Okay, how is it that your daughter is also trapped in the cave but has never looked up once. You've been hanging over her for three straight episodes.'
jokes.setup: They're watching Sunrise Bay again; the show resumes.
jokes.punchline: Alexis applies the same logical scrutiny to Sunrise Bay that she applied to the Ouija question — the cave plot hole is enormous and she catalogues it with specific episode count.
jokes.explanation: Good callback to Alexis's earlier soap opera logical scrutiny. 'Three straight episodes' of Moira hanging in a cave unnoticed is a wonderful absurdist detail.

jokes.text: 'Oh, you know, children, this is where the season picks up. When the cave starts talking back to me. Voiced by the wonderful Peter Falk.'
jokes.setup: Alexis and the audience are skeptical of the cave plot.
jokes.punchline: Moira dismisses the plot hole by revealing the cave talks back — voiced by Peter Falk. The name drop adds a layer of absurdist credibility to completely insane soap opera plotting.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional closing joke. The escalation of Sunrise Bay absurdism culminates in a talking cave voiced by Peter Falk — a real, prestigious actor being deployed in the most ridiculous possible context. The confidence of the name drop ('the wonderful Peter Falk') is perfect Moira. High originality and character integration. A memorable episode capper.

jokes.text: 'I was gonna go for a run.' / 'Shh. She's about to look up.'
jokes.setup: Alexis tries to leave and actually go for her run.
jokes.punchline: Moira shushes her for the key soap opera moment — reversing their earlier dynamic and proving Moira is now more invested in Sunrise Bay than Alexis.
jokes.explanation: Perfect episode button. The reversal is clean — Alexis wanted to watch from the start, Moira was indifferent, and now their positions have swapped completely. 'She's about to look up' implies they've been waiting three episodes for this moment. Great structural close.

tmdb_overview: Moira’s former "Sunrise Bay" producers show up with big news, while Alexis binge-watches old episodes of the soap and discovers some dirt.
insight: Series finale balances character arcs over laughs: 1.94 jokes per minute, the season's lowest density.
